David.

Fabulous.
Now.
We're in public session now.
Just bear with us.
Ah, here we go. Oh,
I think they're on.
Morning, everyone.
Good morning,
Andy.
I'm Alex.
Oh, Alex. Sorry.
Yeah.
You're labeled as Andy.
Right.
We're doing a double act.
Welcome to the Primary Production Select Committee.
You have
five minutes
for your presentation. Take your
submissions read.
So I'll give you a little bit of time to do your
introductory comments, and then if you leave time for questions, that'd be really
useful. So it's in your hands.
Away you go.
Okay. Yeah, good morning, everyone. My name's Alex.
And my name is Andy, and we're bringing you an important message from the future.
But before we move forward in time, it's important to move back.
I'd first of all like to paint a picture for you of our youth.
We grew up in a land where the oceans were plentiful.
We caught fish on every trip, some we kept and many we put
back. These were sort of amazing days spent with family
and friends. We caught fish, lobster, crab,
often ending up on the beach in the evening beside a
beachside fire.
But that was 35 years ago, and it wasn't in New Zealand.
We grew up in Guernsey. Guernsey's a small island in the
English Channel.
Back then, it was a fishing mecca where people would come to go fishing and
leave raving about the Channel Islands and the amazing
catches they got there.
Now the strange thing is, back in our 20s, we started to notice a
decline in the fish we were catching. Fewer fish and smaller fish.
Meanwhile, out to sea, the commercial fleet were working hard.
They were gillnetting and trawling, and it seemed fairly obvious to us that there
was a decline. And talking to the old boys, they told
us tales of even more astonishing abundance.
So clearly this decline wasn't a new thing.
However, what we heard from the commercial fishing industry was calm assurance.
"Don't worry, all is well," they told us.
"We're fishing sustainably, trust us. Why do we want to overfish?
It's our livelihood.
We've run the numbers and we've engaged the scientists and the stocks are perfectly
healthy and there are plenty more fish in the sea."
So you can see the startling similarity with what we heard 30 years ago and what
we're hearing in New Zealand now. Once again, we're being told the seas are
healthy, when to all of us it seems they're not.
We've been presented with a Fisheries Amendment Bill that will prioritize exports,
profit, and private interests, a bill that will certainly accelerate
the take of ocean resources whilst diminishing the opportunity for any of us
to rally against it. Now here's the interesting thing.
What if we could jump forward 30 years and see what the effects of massive
overfishing can do to an ecosystem, a population, and an
entire economy?
Well, we can't look at New Zealand right now, but we can look at Guernsey and
the rest of Europe. How have their oceans fared in the last 30 years
since? Unfortunately, not very well at
all.
I visited the island again last year and the seas are virtually barren.
I dived the same areas that I dived in my youth and it's
completely changed. I was gutted.
Practically any fish of edible size has gone, both the high value
and the low value species.
Now is this what will happen in New Zealand? It's very hard to say.
But I think one thing we can be assured of is that this Fisheries
Amendment Bill will increase the likelihood of that happening here.
And it seems to me we've gone beyond the canary in the coal mine stage.
We've already fished out the northeast coast scallops and the crayfish, the
Hauraki mussels have gone. The Hapuka are only seen
offshore in small numbers and many species are well below
50% of their original biomass. So do you see a pattern
emerging here? Can you see the similarities with Guernsey and many other
ocean nations around the world?
But it's important also to look beyond the sea life and look to the people, those
who enjoy the ocean and the businesses that depend upon it.
Back in Guernsey, the bays which once bustled with small boats are now quiet.
Many of the tackle shops and marine stores, they went out of business.
And our friends and family who all used to go fishing and boating together no
longer bother. What's the point of going out to sea when there's no fish to catch?
And remember the social impact of this.
All those fabulous days that we spent as kids boating, fishing,
barbecuing our fresh catch and sharing it with our families.
These are no longer a thing and a whole way of life has been completely
extinguished.
And there's one more point I'd like to make, that if this bill is to be
decided on economics alone,
then know this, the Guernsey fishing industry has collapsed
despite their unfounded assurances that all would be okay.
And where do the people of Guernsey now go to buy their fish?
Well, they go to the supermarket and yes, you've guessed it, they buy hoki
from New Zealand.
We realize we're not in Guernsey or Europe.
We're on the other side of the world, but there are many, many parallels with other
ocean nations who've suffered systemic declines in marine
species. So you, the decision makers, have a chance here
today to reverse this trend. So we implore you to vote for the
people.
And vote for the ocean
And vote for a better future than the one that we have already experienced.
We believe that we need to vote, and you need to vote to kill the entire Fisheries
Amendment Bill. Thank you.
Thank you.

Thank you.

have that accountability of having camera access
onboard ships.

the multi-year catch decision five-year system is too inflexible for
what we
require. We need to be able to respond to the scientific data
and what that tells us about our

all the surrounding areas. Doing this would benefit everyone, hobby fishermen,

Okay.

to adhere to the UN target of 30% protected oceans by

what you're not happy with, with what has been proposed?

the potential for fines for misuse of camera

information given that what is being fished

150 years ago. And in fact, back then,
by killing whales, we were actually harming ourselves

in

phytoplankton, and they bring it up and they come to the

And that fecal matter fertilizes the phytoplankton at

And that
phytoplankton provides food for
the krill, and so the cycle goes on.
And
all the understanding of this trophic cascade,
it hasn't really been understood or explored at all much

Act in future will take into account marine science such as

I find
in the bill there are many other flaws, and

Hi. My name is Mae. I'm nine, and I'm from Whakatane.
I think it would be dumb if this bill comes in.
Our oceans are already in a terrible state.
We should be protecting them, not making it even easier for fishing boats to
ruin them. We're on track to a dead ocean.
Do we want to make it worse? What I don't like about this
bill is that the Minister of Fisheries can make decisions about how much the
fishing boats are allowed to catch, and the environment is not really the main
thing that is considered when making those decisions.
Penalties for bad practice have also been lowered.
I also don't like that when there are cameras on the boat, the footage won't
really be able to be used, and those who share it can be fined
heaps. We need camera footage to be accessible,
because if the fishing companies know the footage won't be available, they'll
ignore it and do what they like.
A while ago, the Ministry for Primary Industries went through some
footage, and found that there had been much more dolphins, whales, and other
animals killed by fishing boats than fishing
companies said.
We know these commercial fishing boats lie.
Why would we make it even easier for them to do so?
Also, if there are plenty of cameras on the boats with easily accessed footage,
it will make it safer for people on the fishing boats who get abused.
This is a very big problem on fishing boats.
And New Zealand is the only country in the world that allows bottom trawling in the
South Pacific. Bottom trawling is like bulldozing the ocean floor.
It destroys everything in its path, including coral.
Coral takes a really, really long time to grow and will take a long
time to grow back, all for the sake of money.
Allowing fishing companies more control over how they fish will likely lead to
more bottom trawling. In New Zealand, we claim to be clean and green.
This is a lie. Is the government trying to destroy the ocean?
We need to be prioritizing the environment and the world generations
after you're living, not prioritizing a few rich guys' pockets.
We need to object this bill entirely. We also need to ban bottom trawling.
Shouldn't we be ashamed to be the last country bottom trawling in the South
Pacific?
As a vegan, I don't think we should have any commercial fishing in any part of our
oceans. But I know that won't happen, so I suggest that we do what the UN and
heaps of charities and scientists say we should do, and protect at least
30% of it. In NZ, we protect about 1% at the moment.
We need to defend our ocean, and if we work together and do our bit, we can
save the ocean. Thank you for listening.

Tena koutou. Mōrena. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today.
Recently, I watched my lovely five-year-old daughter catch her first
fish. It was a small one, although you wouldn't know it from the retelling of our
stories. Each time the story is told, it grows a little bigger.
The story's a little more magical.Now that moment mattered and I want that
same moment, that same connection to our oceans to be there for my
grandchildren and for theirs. We are not users and takers of these resources.
We are kaitiaki. We need to protect them.
I want to acknowledge the importance of this bill, and New Zealand's export
economy does rely heavily on our fishing industry, and I'm actually fully
supportive of a thriving, successful sector, but the success
must be sustainable. I am concerned that the bill reduces transparency
and oversight and shifts the focus towards short-term gains from
commercial fishing. I support a strong and thriving industry,
but not at the cost of future fish stocks and not at the cost of my children's
opportunity to fish.
Sustainability is not an opposition to economic success, it's what
enables it. If we take too much, too fast or without the right
safeguards, we don't just risk the environment, we risk the long-term viability of
the industry itself, and we risk something that's harder to measure, the
connection between our people and the ocean and between people and the environment.
New Zealand has an opportunity here.
Why can't we lead with an approach that is innovative, future-focused and
genuinely sustainable? An approach that supports the fishing industry
to adapt, evolve and lead rather than prioritize short-term financial
gains. Sustainability should not be seen as a constraint.
It should be seen as a driver of innovation.
So all I wish to leave you with is this: we can fish for today or we can
fish for tomorrow. We can take what we can or we can take what we
should. If we get this right, we don't have to choose between the economy and the
environment. We can protect both.
I urge the committee to ensure that this bill, if it continues,
supports a fishing industry that is not just successful but is sustainable
for our generations to come.

Tena koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to speak
today. I should clarify from the outset that my interest in this
bill is driven solely by its environmental and public
accountability implications. I'm shocked by the legislation
with such serious implications for our marine ecosystems
is even being considered.
It is no surprise then that I do not support the Fisheries
Amendment Bill. Further, I express my strong support for
the positions put forward by Legacy and Forest and Bird, whose
submissions highlight fundamental flaws in the Fisheries Amendment
Bill, which I won't be able to cover everything
todayI want to make four key points today.
First, this bill weakens environmental protections at a time when
we need stronger safeguards. New Zealand's marine
ecosystems are already under enormous pressure from climate
change, habitat loss, and decades of
overfishing. Instead of strengthening regulatory
framework, this bill loosens it. It creates pathways
for increased catch, reduced scrutiny, and more
discretionary decision-making, all at the expense of
ecological resilience.
From an environmental perspective,
this is moving in the wrong direction.
Second, it reduces transparency.
One of the most concerning aspects is the shift away from
independent verification and towards more closed-door
processes. Restricting access to onboard camera
reporting requirements undermine the transparency that New Zealanders
have repeatedly said they want in fisheries management.
If the system is functioning well, transparency should
be welcomed, not avoided.
Three, it concentrates power and reduces
democratic oversight. The bill grants the minister
greater authority while reducing the checks and
balances that ensure decisions are grounded in environmental
science and public interest.
This is not a minor procedural adjustment.
It's a structural change that risks sidelining the voices of
communities, scientists, and environmental
advocates.
New Zealanders have consistently shown that they want
sustainable fisheries and strong environmental
protections. This bill moves us further
away from this expectation.
Four, it privileges short-term commercial interests
over long-term public good and environmental
sustainability. Our oceans are a shared resource.
When legislation prioritizes the interests
of a small number of quota holders over the health of marine
ecosystems and the rights of future
generations, such as the gorgeous Mae,
something is fundamentally out of balance.
A sustainable fishery is one where the environment comes
first, because without a healthy ecosystem, there is no fishery
at all.
In closing, I want to leave you with this thought.
Aotearoa's marine environment is one of our greatest taonga.
You have the chance today to protect something that cannot speak for
itself,
but that future generations will depend on.
I hope you choose the path that honors that responsibility.

Yeah. So as my submission says, I'll open by
simply saying, I do not support the Fisheries Amendment Bill
2026 because it quietly shifts control
of a public resource away from the public.
The submitters, or the majority of the submitters I would say, representing
tens of thousands of New Zealanders, reject the bill entirely.
Not because the fisheries don't need improvement, but because this
bill doesn't fix the real problem.
The current law is already strong.
The failure has been in how it's applied.
And instead of improving implementation, this bill
rewrites the rules, so past failures become
acceptable. Now here's the important part.
You can't understand this bill by looking at one clause at a time.
And in fact, Miles, you
spoke to some earlier
submitters and asked them, "What aspect of the bill do you not like?"
It's the whole thing, because the changes seem technical, but they form a
pattern. Environmental protections are narrowed, public
input is reduced, accountability is weakened, and
legal challenge becomes harder. Taken together, this is
what's known as regulatory capture, where the industry being
regulated gains influence over the rules themselves.
Okay? Take a look at what gets overlooked.
The bill limits how environmental factors are considered when setting catch
limits. It focuses on large management areas, which can
hide local depletion. So while numbers may look stable on paper,
entire local ecosystems can be quietly degrading.

reporting.

So
to give more power to an already deceitful industry, to be

I think it's up to you politicians to prove to us people of New Zealand that
common sense is still there,
and that you guys are going to listen to the people and
veto this bill.
That's all I've got to say. Thank you.

Todd Stevenson.

At best, it's a guess.

Thanks.
Awesome. Cheers.

Awesome. Thank you for your time, and thank you for letting me speak.
No, thank you.
Great.
Cheers.

I do strongly oppose the changes.
Some of the
main reasons for that are the

the fisheries, the way in which the fisheries management system works, in which

Of which
it very much is very
by its nature linked together. So if we

The way in which we currently

So from that perspective, in terms of the
way in which fish are caught,
I
don't agree with it whatsoever. There's much better practices
which can be employed
to get a greater return on those fish as well, and we also don't
impact on other species which get caught up
in bottom trawling.
The other thing too, I think, which is some of the arguments

fishing industry
having a greater say in terms of the management of the fishery.
Well,
look, that's pretty much putting the fox in charge of the henhouse.
You could argue from the fishery side of things that,
yes, look, we'd be wanting to make sure it's sustainable
because of the actual long, the inputs that we have from an

regards to the resources that we've put into large fishing boats,

The problem with that is that as

We tend to have a quick turnaround in terms of return on our own

my thoughts are that
it's better left to the people and to those
successive
people that inherit the actual resource, which is

The fact that there was a
suggestion that
cameras are removed from commercial boats, to me,
again is
in terms of the robustness of anything, it is about transparency

More transparency there is, more accountability there is.
So to even think that that was a good idea, I don't
even know how you join those dots to think that's a good idea, to be honest.
The other aspect to it, the socioeconomic
impacts on it and income and employment.
One of the biggest things, I live in Northland.
Now, one of the
biggest-One of the biggest recreational
pastimes of a lot of Northlanders is fishing.
Now, that economic impact on the local
economy in terms of fuel, boats,
fishing equipment, you name it,
reaches far beyond
a
fishing industry where there's a few trawlers
scraping the bottom of the ocean, of which there would be maybe,
I don't know, let's just say there was 100 people employed in total across those

I'm not sure of the exact figures, but what I do know

than what is
received from
commercial fishers.
The
other aspect to this, I suppose,
that I wanted to highlight was around
the type of, I suppose,
fisheries. I just heard the previous submitter talking

It's always about it's an estimate.
And I can tell you from previous experience
way down the line when we used to do
surveys of orange roughy,
hoki fisheries, and lots of other fisheries on various research
boats and also commercial boats, a lot of the information that
was gleaned and gained, which then went towards setting the quota,
was done with using rigorous scientific
method.
But then what would happen is it would get politicized, and often those
minister-- at the time I wasn't that old, but I certainly could

around what happened when the quota
was finally assessed and given to the minister to

Yep.
I really appreciate

So thank you very much.

Welcome, Craig-
Hello
... to the Primary Production Select Committee.
You have five minutes. How you use that time is up to you.
Fire away.
Kia ora koutou. Thank you for the opportunity to address this committee today.
I'm Craig. I'm a proud New Zealander who wants to be able to take my nephews,
my nieces, and their children fishing in the same
waters that I fish in.
I have already submitted my written opposition to this bill.
Today, I want to make three short points, then ask you one honest question.
Point one, this bill legalizes the harvesting of baby fish.
Minimum size limits are not
bureaucracy, they are biology.
A snapper or any fish must reach a certain size before it can breed, even
once. Take a fish before that size and you remove its entire
future of spawning.
This bill, as drafted, removes that protection
for commercial fishers.
Excuse me.
Recreational fishers like myself still have to follow size limits.
The science is clear, fish need to breed.
But this bill carves out a special exemption for the commercial
industry.
The same biology applies to everyone.
You cannot have two different laws of nature.
The minister has since backed down on some species after public outcry,
but the framework remains. The precedent is set.
If you legalize the killing of juvenile fish, you are harvesting
the nursery or the kōhanga.
As one critic says,
"The size limit clause was just the clickbait.
The rest of the bill remains and the precedent is set.
Once the kōhanga is empty, there's no coming back."
Point two, the fine on camera footage hides the truth from
New Zealanders.
Onboard cameras were introduced for transparency.
They were meant to show what actually happens on commercial fishing vessels, the
bycatch, the dumping, the waste. This bill imposes a
$50,000 fine on anyone who releases that footage
to the public.
If the industry is doing nothing wrong, why hide the evidence?
Sealing those cameras protect lawbreakers, not fish stocks.
It locks the public out of a conversation we have every right to be part of.
This is not accountability. This is a secrecy clause, plain and
simple.
Point three, the 20-day appeal window makes legal challenges
impossible. The bill restricts legal challenges to fisheries
decisions to just 20 working days.
Christ. Official Information Act requests often take longer than 20
days just to receive a response.
How can any citizen, any environmental group, any Maori
collective gather evidence, understand a decision, and
mount a legal challenge in that timeframe?
This provision does not improve the law.
It removes the public's right to hold the government
accountable.Finally, a question,
and this is the heart of my submission.
What I cannot understand is this, why are such
significant changes to our fishery laws being introduced as amendments
rather than standalone legislation?
These are not minor technical fixes.
They remove size limits, they reduce fines, they restrict legal
challenges, they criminalize transparency.
If these changes are good for New Zealand, if they truly protect
sustainability while modernizing the industry,
why are they being bundled together and rushed
through without proper separate scrutiny?
Now, I'm not saying this is bad governing, but good governing does not rush changes
this significant.
Here is the fact, the commercial fishing industry has a long history of
bycatch, illegal dumping, and stock collapse.
The minister himself has acknowledged that trust in fisheries management
is broken, and yet this bill reduces accountability,
hides evidence, weakens penalties.
When you reduce accountability in a low trust industry, you do not fix the
problem, you enable it.
Now, a good government does not hide evidence behind a $50,000
fine, and a good government does not make it harder for its own people to hold
it to account.
And in closing, I'm not asking you to kill this bill because I'm angry, I'm
asking you to kill this bill because I care.
I care about the industry, I care about the fishers who do the right thing
every day,
and I care about my nephews, all our moko, who deserve to fish
in the same waters we did as we grew up.
So please, do not pass this bill. Do not let short-term
profit destroy a resource that belongs to us all.
Consult properly, get this right. Kia ora.
Thank you very much, Craig. We've got enough time for one quick question.
Teanau.
Uh, chairman. Craig, thank you very much for a very strong submission and

as well. And I completely agree with what you're saying.
I was wondering, if there
was going to be, in the future, a Fisheries Amendment Bill, what
would be the top thing or the top two things you think should be in
it?
Sorry, you keep breaking up quite badly.
But I understand you say what do I think should be in
a future fisheries bill?
Yeah. In a bill which you could support.
Yeah, look,
how it is today,
look, nothing's perfect, but the evidence is clear that

it works.
Now,
the minister mentioned something about being able to walk across

There's only one reason why that happened, is because of the management and the

So that's all I say.
If it works, what we should be doing is massaging

a decision that, or come to
an idea, that any of these things actually help

accept history, look at the management processes that worked,

Thanks very much, Craig. We've come well over

It was very comprehensive.

Ben Chisel.

Much like rugby in New Zealand. You've got the wealthy teams from Ponsonby,

rugby and it's very much the same
with
fisheries. It's not like horse racing where it's refined to

was
established and I already had a base of over 100-something thousand at the

I guess the overarching thing is that this
industry, more than any other industry in New Zealand, for as long as it's been

significant amount.
My mother-in-law was one of three people who was given three days in

She will quite happily tell you it was great then,

day,
it was the Wild West. They had no time to do anything.
They were given three days and a hotel in Wanaka in their
mid-30s to write the QMS, and that's how it came about.
And now you've got
Minister Jones
claiming that he's going to make it better
is
quite questionable seeing where those donations come from.
So I guess in terms of me, some suggestions
for how it could be better. One, throw it out, start again.
And when you're going to do that, I think you need more freedom
for the independent fishermen. I've met with Seafood New Zealand, I've talked with
a lot of smaller commercial fishermen, and they are so
scared to say anything that they disagree with because they'll get their
quota or their ace taken off them.
Give them some freedom to make a little bit more
money. But the QMS and the quota holders are
holding them to ransom.
They deem where they can fish. They deem what they will pay them.
They've got no option out there.
The issue with the undersized fish,
it's a tricky one, I will admit.
If trawling is going to continue, we would rather those
fish be kept. Okay? Now that's how this was being
pitched to the public by the minister, but that is not the
case. With trawling, anything you bring on board dies. Okay?
So if the small snapper are going to be there, might as well keep them.
But this bill doesn't-allow for that.
It allows for the fishermen to dump those fish if they want to, or
keep them if they want to. And if they do dump them,
the minister can give allowance to
make up for loss of income or whatever the terminology was around
that. So there is actually no incentivization for
trawlers
to stop doing what they're doing, because they can keep it if they want, they can
throw it if they want, and if they throw it, they'll get given more quota anyway.
It doesn't do anything.
So
there's no reason why the public should
trust the fishing sector,
particularly the big quota holders.
So to remove OIA
requests to find people,
to
get rid of all these extra rules and say, "Trust us," show
us why, because there's no reason.
Okay, thank you, Ben. We've gone well over time, but I really
appreciate your perspective.
And thank you very much for your time.
No problem.
Now, did we get a hold of
Bronson? Right, our next submitter is
Bronson Ata.
Hello, can you hear me?
Morning, Bronson. How are you?
Yeah, good thanks. Good. Apologies for the technical difficulty.
That's all right. It happens to us all.
Yeah.
So thanks for making yourself available.
You have five minutes. Fire away.
Right. Good morning, and thank you for the opportunity to speak today.
My name is Bronson Ata, and I've spent much of my life in and
around the ocean.
I've worked on fishing boats in and around the marine industry for more than
15 years.
And I've spent countless hours diving and fishing around New Zealand.
While diving for scallops, I've personally watched dredgers sweep across the sea
floor and destroy the environment beneath them.
I've seen areas that once held life and abundance become increasingly barren
over time.
My family went to the same crayfish nest for decades.
My dad taught me to always leave the biggest crayfish in the nest because he
believed it helped keep the nest healthy.
That knowledge wasn't written in regulations.
It was stewardship passed down through generations.
Eventually, a commercial cray pot was placed on that nest,
and the big crayfish was taken.
Many years later, that nest still hasn't properly recovered.
That experience has stayed with me because it showed me how easy it is to lose
something that can take decades to rebuild.
I've also seen people exploiting the current system by making multiple trips per
day and taking far more than what most New Zealanders would consider reasonable
or sustainable.
From my perspective, those experiences show that aspects of the current system
are not working as well as they should.
I understand there are competing interests, and I don't pretend
that the answers are simple.
Commercial fishing is important for jobs, exports, food supply, and
regional communities. This should not become
recreational fishers versus commercial fishers.
But recreational fishers deserve to be recognized as part of the solution.
Some of the most knowledgeable and conservation-minded people I know are
recreational fishers. They spend thousands of hours on the water.
They notice changes early. They pass knowledge down through
generations, and many genuinely care about leaving the
fishery better than they found it.
That stewardship matters.
I grew up believing the ocean was something special, a place where families could
gather food together and experience the kind of connection to nature that makes New
Zealand unique.
Because people protect what they feel connected to.
Healthy fisheries also support a huge wider marine economy, from boat
builders and marine servicing through to tourism, hospitality,
fuel sales, accommodation providers and skilled marine trades.
New Zealand should be building world leading fisheries based on abundance,
quality, tourism, marine innovation and long-term value,
not simply extraction volume alone.
But what I've seen over time is gradual depletion, less abundance,
smaller expectations and behavior that the current rules don't
discourage strongly enough.
I think many ordinary New Zealanders feel like they're watching gradual
decline become normalized.
And I don't think we should accept that as inevitable.
New Zealand should be aiming for abundance again, where marine life is thriving,
ecosystems are healthy, and future generations can experience some of the same
richness many older New Zealanders took for granted.
That means protecting sensitive habitats better, recognizing the
long-term value of a healthy inshore fisheries, and strengthening,
not reducing, meaningful consultation and inclusion of
recreational fishers in stewardship and decision making.
Because moving decision making further away from the people most connected to these
fisheries risks losing generations of practical knowledge, stewardship
and experience.
I've spoken to many highly experienced people across the marine industry and
recreational sector, and I struggle to find anyone who genuinely
believes this bill moves us towards more abundant and resilient
fisheries.
I believe New Zealand has an opportunity to lead the world in modern fisheries
management, balancing sustainability, commercial success,
recreation, tourism, marine innovation and long-term abundance
together.
But respectfully, I do not believe this bill in its current form moves us towards
that future. Because
ultimately, these fisheries are not just an economic resource, they are part of
our national identity, our lifestyle, and something we
hold in trust for future generations.
Thank you for your time.
Thank you very much, Bronson, for a
well-thought-out submission and well articulated.
Do we have any questions?
Right. We've come to time anyway. So,
as I said, thank you very much. We really appreciate it.
Great. Thank you.
We'll hear next team. ...
of
the submissions. There's a number
who had indicated they were

So the committee will--
Well, we're not going to suspend. We're going to

carry on at 1:00 p.m.
with the next lot of hearings. And then, of course, we're all
entitled to go on that committee as well, if we have
time.
Thank you, Mr. Chair.
